result.markdown:
--- title: "Using duckdb For Overture Maps" code-fold: false code-overflow: scroll code-annotations: hover --- # Overview [Overture Maps](https://overturemaps.org/) is a free and open map data which distribute its data as GeoParquet files. Those files are hosted on Amazon S3 and Microsoft Azure Blob Storage. Unlike Open Street Maps, which based on updates from the community, Overture Maps based on OSM, governmental and cooperates data with algorithmically AI updates. This difference is significance in the features' meta-data which is much more vast in OSM (although not always accurate) while Overture Maps uses structured schema which gives it much more stability. There are few ways to access the data: * [Overture Maps Explorer](https://explore.overturemaps.org/) where you can browse, search, inspect and download the data. * Overture's Python client which provide command-line interface and a Python library to download the data * Duckdb - an analytical tool which allow you to query and download remote data files with SQL syntax. In this part I'll focus on duckdb's Python's library as a quarrying tool. # Setting ::: {#d23b03cc .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Loading libraries import geopandas as gpd import duckdb ``` ::: ::: {#95ed4b02 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Create an in-memory database con = duckdb.connect() ``` ::: ::: {#7ab9a2dc .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Load duckdb extensions # Geospatial extension that adds support # for working with spatial data and functions con.sql("INSTALL spatial; LOAD spatial") # Adds support for reading and writing files # over a HTTP(S) connection con.sql("INSTALL httpfs; LOAD httpfs") ``` ::: ::: {#a3fe0ffa .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Configure s3 access con.sql("SET s3_region = 'us-west-2';") ``` ::: ::: {#e4dd37fe .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Find the latest Overture Maps release con.sql(""" SET VARIABLE latest_release = ( SELECT latest FROM 'https://stac.overturemaps.org/catalog.json'); """) # Show the latest release con.sql("SELECT getvariable ('latest_release');").show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ┌───────────────────────────────┐ │ getvariable('latest_release') │ │ varchar │ ├───────────────────────────────┤ │ 2026-04-15.0 │ └───────────────────────────────┘ ``` ::: ::: # Querying ## Address Structure First we'll understand the structure of Overture Maps' address on 's3': `s3://overturemaps-us-west-2/release/[latest_release]/theme=[theme]/type=[type]/*` * `[latest_release]`: The latest release of the dataset, as been defined by the SQL variable above (Note: Update once a month) * `[theme]` & `[type]`: The theme and type of the data need to be query, according to the Overture Maps' [schema](https://docs.overturemaps.org/schema/) ## The Query ::: {#c496362c .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 1. Define theme and type of data (Based on OTM schema) theme = "base" ot_type = "land_use" # (Note: don't use 'type' as variable name # to prevent confusion with the 'type' function) # 2. Spatial query rel = con.sql(f""" SELECT id, class, subtype, geometry FROM read_parquet( format('s3://overturemaps-us-west-2/release/{{}}/theme={theme}/type={ot_type}/*', getvariable('latest_release')), filename = true, hive_partitioning = 1) WHERE -- Step 1: Use the bbox as first "Coarse" filter bbox.xmin <= 34.9351 AND bbox.xmax >= 34.8617 AND bbox.ymin <= 32.1834 AND bbox.ymax >= 32.1311 AND -- Step 2: Use ST_Intersects as second filter -- for rows that pass Step 1 (The "Fine" filter) ST_Intersects( geometry, ST_MakeEnvelope(34.8617, 32.1311, 34.9351, 32.1834)); """) ``` ::: # Converting to GeoDataFrame The `geometry` format in duckdb is a binary, which GeoPandas do not recognize. This means that the data should be converted. ## Using 'Arrow' The easiest way is using 'Arrow' as a converter: ::: {#aa650cac .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} arrow_df = rel.arrow() gdf_from_arrow = gpd.GeoDataFrame.from_arrow(arrow_df) gdf_from_arrow.head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=15} ```{=html}
id class subtype geometry
0 f379d527-213f-3175-ae96-176e94da7237 residential residential MULTIPOLYGON (((34.81397 32.12596, 34.81393 32...
1 efd87006-b74c-3a4d-a8e0-b85b5f5f7cd0 residential residential POLYGON ((34.7956 32.14529, 34.80244 32.14402,...
2 164509da-57f1-3031-8a1c-c70f7ed9335c residential residential POLYGON ((34.93882 32.17465, 34.93831 32.17539...
3 fb472082-4c23-33a8-be91-7815769fa5ad farmland agriculture POLYGON ((34.93302 32.17088, 34.93498 32.16903...
4 4ee1058c-7498-38ae-8916-df2405224bff farmland agriculture POLYGON ((34.93771 32.17598, 34.93755 32.17603...
``` ::: ::: ## Using DataFrame ::: {#8f062170 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Convert the GEOMETRY type to WKB (standard BLOB) pd_df = con.sql(""" SELECT * EXCLUDE (geometry), ST_AsWKB(geometry) as geom_binary FROM rel; """).df() # Cast the BLOB the python bytes pd_df['geom_binary'] = pd_df['geom_binary'].apply(bytes) # Load into GeoDataFrame gdf_from_df = gpd.GeoDataFrame( pd_df.drop(columns=['geom_binary']), geometry=gpd.GeoSeries.from_wkb(pd_df['geom_binary']), crs="EPSG:4326" ) gdf_from_df.head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=16} ```{=html}
id class subtype geometry
0 f379d527-213f-3175-ae96-176e94da7237 residential residential MULTIPOLYGON (((34.81397 32.12596, 34.81393 32...
1 efd87006-b74c-3a4d-a8e0-b85b5f5f7cd0 residential residential POLYGON ((34.7956 32.14529, 34.80244 32.14402,...
2 164509da-57f1-3031-8a1c-c70f7ed9335c residential residential POLYGON ((34.93882 32.17465, 34.93831 32.17539...
3 fb472082-4c23-33a8-be91-7815769fa5ad farmland agriculture POLYGON ((34.93302 32.17088, 34.93498 32.16903...
4 4ee1058c-7498-38ae-8916-df2405224bff farmland agriculture POLYGON ((34.93771 32.17598, 34.93755 32.17603...
``` ::: :::
